title: The Dual-Use Problem
concept: The same generative technology that enables creative tools and accessibility features can also be misused to create deceptive or harmful content.

exercises.prompt: Which of these everyday objects best represents a 'dual-use' tool?

options.text: A hammer (used to build a house or break a window)
A puzzle piece (which only fits in one specific place)
A standard key (which only opens one specific door)

exercises.explain: A hammer is a classic 'dual-use' physical tool—it can build a shelter, but it can also cause damage. Generative AI is the digital equivalent.

cards.title: A Tool with Two Edges
cards.body: Imagine designing a super-tool that can write stories, paint pictures, and write code in seconds. It sounds amazing, right? But what if someone else uses that same tool to write fake articles and create deceptive images? This is the double-edged sword of AI.

cards.title: What is the Dual-Use Problem?
cards.body: The 'Dual-Use Problem' refers to the fact that a single AI technology can be used for both beneficial purposes (like helping people write or draw) and harmful purposes (like creating fake news or scam emails). The core technology remains the same; only the user's intent changes.

cards.title: The Kitchen Knife Analogy
cards.body: Think of a kitchen knife. In the hands of a chef, it cuts vegetables to make a delicious soup. In the hands of someone else, it can be used as a dangerous weapon. The knife itself isn't good or bad; it is designed for cutting, and how it is used depends entirely on the person holding it.

cards.title: Voice Cloning Dual-Use
cards.body: An AI voice cloner can help a person who lost their voice speak again with their natural-sounding voice. However, that exact same voice cloner can be used by bad actors to mimic someone's relative and trick them into sending money over the phone.

cards.title: Myth: Bad AI is Always Made by Bad People
cards.body: Many people believe that if an AI tool does something bad, the technology must have been designed by evil engineers. In reality, most AI models are designed to be general helpers. The harms often come from users exploiting the tool's open-ended capabilities in ways the creators did not intend.

cards.title: One Tool, Two Paths
cards.body: Depending on who uses the AI tool and how, the exact same system can produce vastly different outcomes for society.

left.text: Creating custom illustrations for a children's storybook.

right.text: Generating realistic fake photos to spread rumors online.

exercises.prompt: Why is it so difficult for AI creators to prevent their tools from ever being misused?

options.text: AI creators always want their tools to be used maliciously.
The same underlying technology can be applied to both helpful and harmful tasks.
AI systems are alive and choose to behave badly on their own.
It is physically impossible to write helpful software.

exercises.explain: AI tools are flexible and general-purpose, meaning the creator cannot fully control how a user might apply the technology once it is released.

exercises.prompt: True or False: An AI tool designed to help artists create digital paintings can also be used to generate deceptive fake photos.
exercises.explain: The dual-use problem exists because the same helpful tool (like an image generator) can be redirected to create deceptive or harmful images.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe technology that can be used for both helpful and harmful goals.
exercises.sentence: Generative AI is considered a [blank] technology because it can serve both good and bad purposes.
exercises.explain: Because generative AI has two potential paths of application—one positive and one negative—it is classified as a dual-use technology.

exercises.prompt: Which of these scenarios represents a harmful misuse of AI text generation?

options.text: A teacher using AI to draft simple reading comprehension quizzes for students.
A competitor using AI to generate thousands of fake negative reviews for a local bakery.
A programmer using AI to find spelling mistakes in their code comments.

exercises.explain: Generating fake reviews to manipulate buyers is a deceptive misuse of AI text generation. The other options show helpful, constructive uses of the same technology.

exercises.prompt: Why do we refer to generative AI as a 'dual-use' technology?

options.text: The AI code is broken and functions incorrectly half the time.
The AI is smart enough to choose whether it wants to be good or bad.
The same basic generation technology can be used for both helpful and harmful purposes.
The AI is designed to only work for two specific companies.

exercises.explain: AI technologies are inherently dual-use because they are general-purpose tools. The same pattern-matching and generation code that helps a user write an email or create digital art can also be used to write spam or generate deceptive images. Preventing misuse requires intentional safety guardrails.
